signature: Orbits of an integer map that multiplies by ~3/2 and branches on parity; open questions are exact-boundary/exact-hit properties of the resulting deterministic symbol stream (halting counter hitting a boundary against drift, hitting an exact power of 2); certificates = invariant structures of a rational transduction. Hunted keyword-free across: algorithmic verification/logic (Skolem-Positivity, loop termination), Diophantine dynamics of (3/2)^n, base-2/base-3 digit rigidity, wqo/well-structured transition systems, analytic combinatorics of lattice paths, alpha-continued-fraction matching, bounded arithmetic with counting axioms.

finds.ref: K. Mahler, 'An unsolved problem on the powers of 3/2', J. Austral. Math. Soc. 8 (1968), 313-321 (Behrend Memorial Lecture 1966)
finds.the_theorem: Define a Z-number as x>0 with {x(3/2)^n} in [0,1/2) for all n>=0. Mahler proves: the integer parts g_n = [x(3/2)^n] of any Z-number satisfy the parity-branched recursion g_{n+1} = (3g_n + eps_n)/2 with eps_n = g_n mod 2 (his eq. (2)), i.e. g_{n+1} = ceil(3g_n/2); each unit interval [m, m+1) contains at most one Z-number; and #{Z-numbers <= x} = O(x^0.7). His 'break-off algorithm' (G_k odd, H_k even, 2^{a_k}||H_k, G_{k+1}=(3/2)^{a_k}H_k...) terminates iff no Z-number sits in the interval; he notes 'in spite of much computer work, no integer G_1 is known for which the algorithm does not break off.' (Flatto later improved the exponent to log_2(3/2).)
finds.the_match: This is literally our map family: Mahler's recursion g -> (3g+parity)/2 = ceil(3g/2) is the mirror of Antihydra/Hydra's floor(3n/2) (conjugate under n -> -n / unit offset), and Z-number existence = 'the deterministic parity itinerary avoids a forbidden congruence event forever', exactly the Antihydra exact-boundary question with polarity flipped. The counting bound x^0.7 comes from constraint propagation: each itinerary step halves the density of admissible seeds while the reachable state space multiplies by 3/2. GAP, flagged: Mahler's forbidden event is an INSTANTANEOUS condition ({x(3/2)^n} crossing 1/2, equivalently a parity/divisibility break at step n), while Antihydra's boundary is a CUMULATIVE functional of the itinerary (counter 2#even - #odd); constraint propagation through a path-integrated condition is genuinely harder. Blindness verified by full-text grep of the Documenta re-typesetting: zero hits for Collatz/Syracuse/3x+1 (1968, pre-dates the printed Collatz literature). Note: another agent in this program has also pulled Mahler (mahler-1968-powers-of-3-2.pdf existed in papers/); my contribution is the explicit conjugacy to the cryptid recursion and the counting-transfer below.
finds.transfer: Port Mahler's seed-counting argument to the cryptids: prove |{n <= x : the Antihydra counter stays in the survival region for >= log_{3/2} x steps under a prescribed itinerary type}| <= x^{theta+o(1)} with theta = log_2 3 - 1, and the same for Space Needle seeds whose (value + v2-valuation) trajectory hits an exact power of 2 late. Concretely: (i) write out the conjugacy between Mahler's break-off algorithm and floor(3n/2)+counter; (ii) rerun his Satz-by-Satz counting with the cumulative constraint replaced by a union over counter levels, yielding the first unconditional density bound on 'late-halting/never-halting' seeds for a BB cryptid.

finds.ref: J. Ouaknine, J. Worrell, 'Positivity problems for low-order linear recurrence sequences', SODA 2014 / arXiv:1307.2779
finds.the_theorem: For integer linear recurrence sequences: (1) Positivity (all terms >= 0) is decidable for order <= 5, within coNP^{PP^{PP^{PP}}}; Ultimate Positivity is decidable in P for order <= 5. (2) Hardness (their Thms 5.1/5.2, verified in the PDF): if (Ultimate) Positivity is decidable at order 6, then for every t in a dense set T of transcendental numbers (t = arg(alpha)/2pi, alpha algebraic on the unit circle), the homogeneous Diophantine approximation constant L_infinity(t) (resp. type L(t)) is computable - a longstanding open problem (e.g. whether L_infinity(pi) is 0, 1/3, or in between). Partial converses hold: undecidability proofs at order 6 would also have Diophantine consequences.
finds.the_match: Shared signature: an exact-boundary question about a deterministic integer stream (does u_n ever cross 0) whose decidability is EQUIVALENT, at the frontier, to computing Lagrange constants/types of specific transcendental numbers via continued fractions. This is the same obstruction that caps Collatz cycle-exclusion (Steiner, Simons-de Weger, Hercher bounds hinge on the continued fraction / irrationality type of log_2 3) - here formalized as a two-way reduction rather than folklore. GAP, flagged: Collatz orbit streams are generated by a piecewise-affine (parity-branched) map, NOT an LRS - characteristic roots 2 and 3 are real, and the branching is the whole difficulty; the match is at the level of the Diophantine obstruction and the decision-theoretic framing, not the dynamics. Full-text grep: zero Collatz/3x+1 mentions.
finds.transfer: Make the folklore two-sided and precise: (a) encode the m-cycle equations for accelerated Collatz as a family of Positivity-style queries and locate their effective 'order'; (b) prove the converse using the OW Section-5 reduction template: a general algorithm deciding 'Collatz has an m-cycle with e evens, o odds for given (e,o)-ranges' computes the Diophantine type L(log_2 3) - i.e., an unconditional 'cycle-decision is Diophantine-hard' theorem mirroring their order-6 hardness, which would explain WHY cycle bounds stall at the strength of Baker/Rhin-type bounds.

finds.ref: M. Hosseini, J. Ouaknine, J. Worrell, 'Termination of linear loops over the integers', ICALP 2019, LIPIcs 132, 118 / arXiv:1902.07465
finds.the_theorem: Theorem 16 (verified in PDF): termination on ALL integer initial values is decidable for single-path affine loops 'while (Bx >= b) do x := Ax + a' over Z^d, for arbitrary (not necessarily diagonalisable) update matrices A - settling Tiwari's 2004 conjecture. Key device (their Thm 14): non-termination iff there is a witness z and a 'dominating' eigenvalue-equivalence class E with inf over the torus closure T(gamma) of Phi_E(z,mu) >= 0. Pointedly, termination on a SINGLE initial value for the same class is open (Skolem/Positivity-hard).
finds.the_match: The decidability frontier they draw is exactly the Collatz silhouette: (i) 'all inputs' questions are tractable while 'single orbit' questions are number-theoretically hard - the same asymmetry as Terras/Tao density results vs. the conjecture itself; (ii) their proof reduces to controlling orbits of (A, a) against a linear boundary via equidistribution on a torus closure (Kronecker/o-minimality) - i.e., drift + equidistribution arguments ARE the decision procedure. GAP, flagged: Collatz/Antihydra are TWO-branch loops with modular (parity) guards, outside the single-path class; multi-path affine loops are undecidable in general (Conway/Kurtz-Simon-type encodings), so any transfer must aim at a thin intermediate class. Full-text grep: zero Collatz mentions (their 'longstanding open problem' reference is Skolem-type, not Collatz).
finds.transfer: Define the class L(par): 1-dimensional loops 'while (n != 1) { if n even n := f0(n) else n := f1(n) }' with f0, f1 affine with multipliers m0, m1 and average log-drift (log m0 + log m1)/2 < 0, and attempt a HOW-style decision procedure for termination-on-all-inputs (equivalently: 'every orbit enters a bounded set') for a subfamily where the parity sequence provably equidistributes (e.g. f0, f1 with 2-adic contraction). Even a decidability result for a nontrivial subfamily would be the first 'drift arguments are a decision procedure' theorem adjacent to Collatz; failure modes would isolate exactly which Diophantine input (type of log_2(m1/m0)) is missing.

finds.ref: C. Banderier, P. Flajolet, 'Basic analytic combinatorics of directed lattice paths', Theoret. Comput. Sci. 281 (2002), 37-80; plus C. Banderier, M. Wallner, 'The kernel method for lattice paths below a line of rational slope', arXiv:1606.08412 (Dev. Math., Springer, 2019)
finds.the_theorem: BF 2002: for directed lattice paths with any finite step set S in Z, the generating functions of walks/bridges/meanders/excursions confined to the half-plane are algebraic, computed exactly by the kernel method (kernel 1 - t*P(u) = 0, small branches u_j(t)), with universal asymptotics rho^{-n} n^{-3/2} (excursions) etc., including periodic-support cases with several dominant singularities. BW 2016 extends enumeration/asymptotics to paths below a line of ARBITRARY rational slope (solving Knuth's AofA 2014 problem for slope 2/5), where exact-boundary contact events carry congruence conditions.
finds.the_match: Antihydra's counter is EXACTLY a directed lattice path with step set {+2 (on even value), -1 (on odd value)} absorbed at an exact boundary; halting = first passage. Under the iid parity null model the kernel equation gives the descent probability q = 1/2 + q^3/2, whose root is q = 1/phi - I verified this reproduces the cryptid's heuristic halting probability (1/phi)^n exactly, and BF gives the full correction: first-passage counts ~ C phi^{-n} n^{-3/2} with computable C, and the congruence-filtered exact-hit counts come from the periodic-support/several-singularities analysis (BW). GAP, flagged and essential: the parity stream is deterministic, not iid; this machinery yields the precise null model and testable predictions, not theorems about the actual orbit of 8. Blindness: BF full text 0 hits; BW's single '3n+1' grep hit is a binomial coefficient C(3n+1, n) fragment - false positive, blind.
finds.transfer: Compute, in closed form via the kernel method, the exact algebraic generating function of Antihydra halting itineraries (steps +2/-1, absorbing boundary, initial counter c0), extract the asymptotic halting probability with its n^{-3/2} polynomial correction and constant, and fit against bbchallenge simulation data of the true deterministic stream; the measured deviation (or agreement) becomes a quantitative pseudorandomness statement about the floor(3n/2) parity stream - the right target for any later derandomization argument. Same program for Space Needle's exact power-of-2 hits using the congruence-filtered (periodic support) version.

finds.ref: C. L. Stewart, 'On the representation of an integer in two different bases', J. reine angew. Math. 319 (1980), 63-72
finds.the_theorem: For multiplicatively independent bases a, b >= 2 there is an effectively computable C(a,b) such that every n > 25 satisfies s_a(n) + s_b(n) > log log n / (log log log n + C) - 1, where s_a is the number of nonzero digits (equivalently: only finitely many, effectively boundable, integers are simultaneously 'digitally sparse' in both bases). Effective version, via Baker's linear forms in logarithms + Loxton-van der Poorten, of the Senge-Straus finiteness theorem. (Cognate rigidity result: Cobham 1969 - a set of integers recognizable by finite automata in two multiplicatively independent bases is eventually periodic.)
finds.the_match: Collatz certificates live exactly at the base-2/base-3 interface: orbit elements are n_k = (3^k n + (correction from the parity word))/2^{s_k}, inverse orbits of 1 are Z[1/3]-combinations of powers of 2, and our proved result ('no regular invariant set with <= 7 DFA states, base 2') is a statement that no base-2-simple structure is T-invariant. Stewart says base-2-sparse and base-3-sparse structure cannot coexist in large integers, EFFECTIVELY; Cobham says base-2-automatic + base-3-automatic = eventually periodic. GAP, flagged: Stewart bounds digit COUNTS, not automatic complexity; invariant sets of T need not contain digitally sparse elements, so a pumping-style argument is needed to force sparse witnesses into any small automaton language. Blindness: the GDZ scan has no OCR layer so I could not full-text grep; verified instead that Stewart 1980 is absent from BOTH of Lagarias's exhaustive annotated 3x+1 bibliographies (1963-1999 and 2000-2009) - strong evidence, flagged as indirect.
finds.transfer: Two-step: (1) clean theorem to attempt: no infinite T-invariant set avoiding 1 is both 2-automatic and 3-automatic - by Cobham reduce to eventually periodic sets, then kill arithmetic progressions directly with the 3-adic/2-adic action of T (this looks provable in a week and generalizes the DFA-state search in a different direction: any state count, but two bases). (2) harder: use Stewart's effective bound to show any base-2 DFA certificate must have >= f(states) elements of unbounded s_3-digit sum, quantifying why the regular-certificate search fails as states grow.

finds.ref: A. Finkel, Ph. Schnoebelen, 'Well-structured transition systems everywhere!', Theoret. Comput. Sci. 256 (2001), 63-92
finds.the_theorem: Verified in PDF: Theorem 4.6 - termination is decidable for any well-structured transition system with (1) transitive compatibility of the wqo with transitions, (2) decidable <=, (3) effective Succ. Theorem 11.3 (completeness of the certificate class): for any finitely-branching effective transition system, the canonical 'termination ordering' <=_T is a compatible wqo, and <=_T is decidable IFF termination is decidable - i.e., every terminating system has a wqo certificate, and decidable wqo certificates are exactly as hard as the termination problem itself.
finds.the_match: Reframes our certificate landscape: a divergence/convergence certificate for Collatz-as-transition-system (halt at 1) is a decidable wqo on N compatible with T, and FS Thm 11.3 says this certificate class is COMPLETE - so, as with regular invariant sets, the only searchable certificates are restricted subclasses. GAP, flagged: compatibility for a deterministic map means monotonicity of (iterates of) T w.r.t. the wqo, and T's parity branching breaks monotonicity for every natural ordering; also Collatz 'termination' requires making T(1) undefined, and FS termination is termination-from-every-state, so partial certificates (invariant subsets) need the coverability variants of their theorems. Full-text grep: zero Collatz mentions.
finds.transfer: Prove a no-certificate theorem in a NEW class, parallel to the 7-state DFA result: no '2-automatic wqo' (well-quasi-order whose relation is recognizable by a k-state synchronous base-2 automaton) with small k is transitively compatible with the accelerated Collatz relation while keeping {1} minimal. The search space is finite for each k (enumerate automatic relations, check wqo-ness via known decidability of wqo for automatic relations, check compatibility on a bounded window plus a pumping argument), so this is machine-checkable in the same style as the DFA search, but certifies against a genuinely different certificate type (orderings, not sets).

finds.ref: C. Carminati, G. Tiozzo, 'A canonical thickening of Q and the dynamics of continued fractions', Ergodic Theory Dynam. Systems 32 (2012), 1249-1269 / arXiv:1004.3790
finds.the_theorem: For Nakada's alpha-continued fraction maps T_alpha: Theorem 1.2 - the matching set M (parameters where the orbits of the two endpoints collide exactly: T_alpha^N(alpha) = T_alpha^M(alpha-1) for finite N, M) is open, dense, of FULL Lebesgue measure, yet its complement has Hausdorff dimension 1. Theorem 1.3 - M is the disjoint union of 'maximal quadratic intervals' I_a indexed by rationals, on each of which the matching exponents (N, M) are constant and the entropy alpha -> h(T_alpha) is monotone. Lemma 2.8 - exceptional (non-matching) parameters are of bounded type (partial quotients bounded by a_1), i.e. badly approximable.
finds.the_match: An exact-hit property of a piecewise ×-branching family: 'do two orbits of the transduction meet EXACTLY' holds generically, is certified by FINITE data (a quadratic interval = a combinatorial certificate valid on a whole parameter interval), and fails precisely on a dimension-1 fractal of badly-approximable parameters - the same picture as OW's Positivity hardness (bounded-type numbers as the obstruction) and as our expectation that cryptid exact-hits are generic but the specific instance (beta = 3/2, seed 8) may sit at an exceptional point. GAP, flagged and large: alpha-CF maps are contracting Gauss-type interval maps with a parameter, while Antihydra is a single expanding integer map with no given parameter family; the transfer requires building the family first, and there is no guarantee the halting set in the parameter is structured like M. Full-text grep: zero Collatz mentions. Note: parallel agents already pulled the adjacent Bruin-Carminati-Marmi-Profeti piecewise-affine matching papers; this find is the theorem-complete source with the bounded-type characterization.
finds.transfer: Embed Antihydra in the one-parameter family H_beta(n) = floor(beta n) (counter +2/-1 on parity), beta in (1,2), and study the halting set in beta for fixed seed: conjecture and try to prove, via the matching-interval machinery, that 'halting holds on an open dense set of beta containing a neighborhood-minus-a-point structure at rationals p/q, with combinatorial certificates constant on intervals'; then locate beta = 3/2 relative to the exceptional set. Even the numerical map of halting intervals in beta would reveal whether 3/2 sits at a matching-interval endpoint (structural reason for hardness) or interior (certificate exists and is findable).

finds.ref: E. Jerabek, 'Approximate counting in bounded arithmetic', J. Symbolic Logic 72 (2007), 959-993
finds.the_theorem: In APC1 := PV1 + dWPHP(PV) (feasible arithmetic plus the dual/surjective weak pigeonhole principle for p-time functions), one can define approximate cardinalities of poly-time (circuit-)definable sets up to polynomially small multiplicative error, prove inclusion-exclusion and Chernoff-type concentration bounds, and formalize reasoning about randomized classes (BPP, APP, MA, AM). I.e., counting/averaging/drift arguments over exponentially large sample spaces become PROOFS in an explicitly calibrated fragment of arithmetic.
finds.the_match: Terras's theorem - density of n with finite stopping time is 1 - is at bottom a Chernoff bound over the 2^k equidistributed parity vectors mod 2^k: 'most parity words of length k have negative log-drift, and the parity map n mod 2^k -> word is a bijection'. Every ingredient (iterating T for k steps on k-bit residues, the bijection, the binomial tail) is poly-time definable, so the drift argument looks like an APC1 proof - answering, for this instance, the wander-prompt 'is there a formal system where drift arguments ARE proofs'. GAP, flagged: Terras's full statement quantifies over all k with density -> 1 (a Pi_2-flavored limit); APC1 should prove the explicit-rate version 'for all k: |{n < 2^k : no drop within k steps}| <= 2^k * 2^{-delta k}' but bootstrapping to Tao-style 'almost all orbits attain almost bounded values' needs iteration schemes likely beyond one Chernoff application. Full-text grep: zero Collatz mentions.
finds.transfer: Prove: the explicit-rate Terras theorem is a theorem of APC1 (or even I-Delta0 + dWPHP), and the correctness proofs of the bbchallenge drift-based deciders formalize in PV1 + dWPHP. Payoff: (a) a proof-complexity HOME for the entire heuristic layer of the program; (b) a sharp independence target - if full Collatz convergence (or Antihydra non-halting) were provable at all, the counting fragment it exceeds becomes measurable; conversely any future 'no small certificate' results (like the 7-state DFA theorem) should formalize in PV1, separating the certificate layer from the drift layer axiomatically.

best_single_bet: Mahler 1968. It is a PROVEN counting theorem about literally our map family: g_{n+1} = (3g_n + parity)/2 = ceil(3g_n/2), the mirror of Antihydra's floor(3n/2), with a break-off event and the exact remark 'no seed is known for which the algorithm does not break off'. First move (day 1-2): write out the explicit conjugacy between Mahler's Z-number algorithm (his G_k/H_k/a_k scheme, eq. (2)) and the Antihydra/Hydra recursion-with-counter, verifying it numerically on bbchallenge data; then (day 3-7) rerun Mahler's density-halving-vs-state-space-times-3/2 counting argument with his instantaneous constraint replaced by the cumulative counter constraint (union over counter levels, one Banderier-Flajolet kernel factor per level), targeting the first unconditional theorem of the form |{seeds n <= x whose itinerary avoids the exact boundary for >= log_{3/2} x steps}| <= x^{theta+o(1)}, theta = log_2 3 - 1 approx 0.585, for Antihydra and for Space Needle's exact power-of-2 hits.
dead_ends: (1) De Gruyter direct PDF for Stewart 1980 is hard-paywalled and the U. Waterloo 'PDF' is a 1-page stub - the working route is EUDML -> GDZ METS file -> per-article LOG_0008 download (now saved); note the GDZ scan has NO OCR layer, so full-text blindness grep is impossible for that paper (verified blindness indirectly via absence from both Lagarias annotated bibliographies). (2) Effective homogeneous-dynamics searches ('effective equidistribution semisimple/unipotent') surface post-2020 homogeneous space technology far from integer maps; the relevant x2-x3 line (Berend 1984, Einsiedler-Lindenstrauss) was ALREADY downloaded by a parallel agent - stood down to avoid duplication, do not re-search. (3) Tropical/max-plus JSR and the constrained-switching JSR lane is likewise already claimed (daviaud-guillon-merlet, philippe-essick-dullerud-jungers files present) - do not re-search. (4) Bruin-Carminati-Marmi-Profeti piecewise-affine matching also already pulled by a parallel agent; my alpha-CF find is the complementary theorem-complete source. (5) Searching analytic combinatorics for 'survival probability' fails - that community says 'meanders/excursions/walks staying positive'; use those terms. (6) The mimuw.edu.pl Finkel-Schnoebelen link 403s without a browser User-Agent (fixed with -A flag). (7) 'Regular separability of WSTS' (Czerwinski et al.) looked promising for the certificate angle but its separators are for VASS-like languages, not orderings on N compatible with a deterministic map - low yield, skipped.
